query: Kawasaki disease symptoms and鉴别诊断

retrieved_docs.title: Kawasaki Disease -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Children with Kawasaki disease should get an echocardiogram at the time of diagnosis, two weeks after symptom onset, and again at eight weeks after the diagnosis.  AHA recommends repeating an echocardiogram 10 to 14 days after the initial one and 4 to 6 weeks after all laboratory data have normalized. The goal of treatment is the prevention of cardiac complications.  Therefore, all patients diagnosed with Kawasaki disease should receive IVIG and aspirin. Many children get lost to long-term follow-up as an adult leading to severe cardiac complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Kawasaki Disease -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Long-term management starts after the acute illness has passed, typically 5-6 weeks after the initial onset of the fever; this is typically when coronary artery involvement has reached its peak in severity.  The frequency of follow-up, medication, and repeat images are on a patient by patient basis and their disease severity. [2] However, both the AHA and Japanese Circulation Society (JCS) recommend routine cardiac stress testing for patients with medium-large CAAs as well as non-invasive imaging. [30] There is no consensus on the duration for ASA treatment, or whether there is any benefit of the patient being on dual anti-coagulant therapy after the acute phase of the disease.  Low-molecular-weight-heparin or warfarin (with a goal of INR 2 to 2.5) is recommended in patients who have large CAAs, as well as an anti-platelet agent (clopidogrel or dipyridamole) to prevent thrombus formation due to decreased flow and the damaged epithelium. [1] [4] Platelet activity remains elevated from 3 months to one year after disease onset, and patients should be on some type of low-dose anti-platelet therapy for 3 months. [4] The overall goal of long-term therapy is to prevent myocardial ischemia and infarction.  Complete resolution of coronary artery lesions has been observed in over 50% of patients 1 to 2 years from the onset of disease and seems to be dependent on the size of the initial lesion. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Kawasaki Disease -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: This disease is most common in children younger than five years of age, but it can present at any age, even in adults. [4] There is a slight male predominance (male to female = 1.5 to 1). [2] [6] [4] Boys are also more likely to suffer from complications and death. [3] It is rare to see Kawasaki disease in children less than 4 months, possibly pointing to protection from maternal antibodies. [1] It is most commonly seen in children of Asian descent, particularly Japanese, and is least common in Caucasian children. [1] There also appears to be a higher prevalence of the disease in the winter and spring months. [1] [3] [4] Incidence varies from 10 to 20 in 100,000 children aged < 5 years in the U.S. and Canada to 50 to 250 in 100,000 in Japan, Taiwan or Korea.

retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Kawasaki Disease -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: A cardiologist should be consulted to determine the initial and schedule of following echocardiogram studies, anticoagulation management, to determine if coronary artery angiography is necessary and for long-term follow-up and monitoring.  Other specialists to consider, include an infectious disease specialist, rheumatologist, or dermatologist. Following treatment, the short-term prognosis is good, but the long-term prognosis remains unknown primarily because many children are lost to followup.

retrieved_docs.title: Kawasaki Disease -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Prognosis depends on the severity of cardiac disease.  Additionally, the prognosis is better for children diagnosed between age 6 months to 9 years than those younger or older, perhaps because they are diagnosed earlier after presenting with classic findings. [35] Recurrence is uncommon but is most likely to occur in younger children who had cardiac complications from the disease during the initial episode.  All KD related deaths are essentially the result of cardiac complications and typically occur 15-45 days from the onset of fever. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Kawasaki Disease -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids have been proposed as part of the initial therapy (in addition to IVIG and ASA) due to findings of decreased risk of developing cardiac abnormalities. However, research results have been inconsistent  The RAISE trial published in 2012 reintroduced the idea of using extended doses of prednisolone (2mg/kg/day for 4 to 5 weeks) in patients with KD who were at high risk for being resistant to IVIG therapy. [28] There have been no findings suggestive that corticosteroids are harmful, and may be a therapeutic option at the discretion of the treating physician.  Other studies suggest corticosteroids reduce the occurrence of CAAs, duration of fever, duration of hospitalization, and time taken to normalization of inflammatory markers. [1] [3] [21] [29] and may be beneficial in cases refractory to initial IVIG therapy. [2] [29] Recommended doses are 2 mg/kg of prednisone or its equivalent for 3 days to 2 weeks, depending on the individual's response. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Kawasaki Disease -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Kawasaki disease is a rare disorder, but because of its serious effects on the coronaries, the condition is best managed by an interprofessional team. Once a child is suspected of having Kawasaki disease, the patient should be referred promptly to a cardiologist.

retrieved_docs.title: Kawasaki Disease -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: A biopsy is rarely performed and is not necessary to make a diagnosis.  Findings early in the disease show destruction of the media of vessels by neutrophils. [8] Coronary arteritis characteristically presents with granulomatous inflammation that develops 6-8 days into the illness and is present across all layers of the vessel.[4] [9] As the disease progresses, the infiltration gets replaced by lymphocytes, monocytes, and fibroblasts which lead to arterial remodeling. [8] Both light and electron microscopy show cytoplasmic inclusion bodies containing RNA in 85% of acute and late-stage fatalities. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Kawasaki Disease -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: If KD is on your differential, order the echocardiogram; missing a diagnosis or diagnosing the disease late can be catastrophic. If considering incomplete KD, order ESR, CRP, CBC, serum albumin and alanine aminotransferase and urinalysis.

retrieved_docs.title: Kawasaki Disease -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Kawasaki disease' presentation also overlaps with other immunologic reactions such as multiple drug hypersensitivity reactions, juvenile idiopathic arthritis, infantile polyarteritis nodosa, and systemic lupus erythematosus. These can be differentiated from KD by the absence of classic clinical criteria and by chronicity and the number of joints affected. [6] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Kawasaki Disease -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Kawasaki disease (KD), also known by the name mucocutaneous lymph node syndrome, is an acute, self-limited medium vessel vasculitis that has a predilection for the coronary arteries. [1] It is the leading cause of acquired heart disease in developed nations and is slowly bypassing rheumatic heart disease in developing countries. [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Kawasaki Disease -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Aneurysms are defined as vessel diameter >3mm in children younger than 5 years old and >4mm in children older than 5 years. [1] Cardiac complications later on in adulthood have been shown to be more common in those patients who had a CAA >6.0mm. [30] Giant aneurysms (>8.0mm) are likely to occlude from a thrombus within in the first year causing an MI. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Kawasaki Disease -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Kawasaki disease (KD) is an acute, self-limited medium vessel vasculitis that has a predilection for the coronary arteries. This activity reviews the evaluation and treatment of Kawasaki disease and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in improving care for patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Kawasaki Disease -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: In those who aneurysms resolve, there will be significant thickening of the vessel leading to an increased risk of premature coronary atherosclerotic disease. [18] In a study done on adults who suffered from an MI later in life after having had a coronary artery aneurysm in childhood due to KD, there was no evidence of remaining aneurysm. [36] However, one cannot assume that just because an aneurysm resolves, that does not mean the risk of cardiac complications later in life also dissipates. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Kawasaki Disease -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who develop severe perfusion deficits from an aneurysm may require coronary artery bypass graft surgery and should be a consideration in children who have reversible ischemia or patients that have recurrent MIs. [1] Percutaneous intervention commonly fails due to the severity of disease and significant narrowing of the arteries, putting the patient at higher risk for new aneurysm formation or perforation. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 1317
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic pancarditis, acute rheumatic valvulitis, التهاب عضل القلب الروماتزمي الحاد (acute rheumatic myocarditis), الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد (acute rheumatic pericarditis), and مرض قلبي روماتزمي حاد، غير محدد (unspecified acute rheumatic heart disease) are all manifestations of acute rheumatic cardiac involvement that share a common clinical course characterized by a sudden onset and/or short duration. This rapid and brief presentation is a key feature for clinicians to recognize when evaluating patients with rheumatic heart disease, as it helps differentiate these acute forms from more chronic rheumatic cardiac conditions and guides timely diagnostic and therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2293
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic carditis, acute rheumatic pancarditis, acute rheumatic valvulitis, الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد (acute rheumatic pericarditis), and الْتِهابُ الشَّغافِ الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحادّ (acute rheumatic endocarditis) are all manifestations of acute rheumatic heart disease that share a common clinical course characterized by a sudden onset and/or short duration. This abrupt and brief presentation is a key feature across these related conditions, aiding clinicians in recognizing the rapid evolution of rheumatic involvement of the heart’s structures and informing timely diagnostic and therapeutic decisions.

Recognizing this rapid presentation across the various forms of acute rheumatic heart disease helps clinicians diagnose and manage these conditions promptly.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 1198

Recognizing this rapid and brief presentation pattern is essential for timely diagnosis and appropriate management of these acute disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1728
retrieved_docs.content: Late effects of cerebrovascular disease has parent اضطراب مخي وعائي, indicating that the long‑term sequelae of cerebrovascular disease are classified under the broader category of cerebrovascular disorder. Recognizing this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians organize diagnoses, anticipate chronic complications, and tailor long‑term management strategies for patients who have experienced cerebrovascular events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 2 -- Community 1079
retrieved_docs.content: Ischemic heart disease (إقفار عَضَلِ القَلْبِ) is described as a central cardiomyopathic disorder that includes coronary artery disease (مرض الشريان التاجي), coronary disease (مرض التاجي), chronic unspecified ischemic heart disease (مرض قلبي إقفاري مزمن، غير محدد) and vascular dilation in the heart (تمدد الأوعية الدموية في القلب). It is classified as a child of myocardial, endocardial, pericardial and valve disorders (MYO ENDO PERICARDIAL & VALVE DISORDERS) and is linked to coronary atherosclerosis, other heart disease and vascular ischemia. Typical manifestations are palpitations (خفقانات), a systolic murmur (النفخة الانقباضية), chest constriction (خُنَاقٌ صَدْرِيّ) and progression to heart failure (فشل قلبي). Management frequently involves coronary artery bypass surgery (مجازة الشريان التاجي), coronary computed‑tomography angiography (تصوير الأوعية التاجية بالفحص المقطعي المحوسب), and the antiplatelet agent tirofiban (including tirofiban hydrochloride) for prevention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1449
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic pancarditis, acute rheumatic valvulitis, acute rheumatic myocarditis (التهاب عضل القلب الروماتزمي الحاد), acute rheumatic pericarditis (الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد), and acute rheumatic endocarditis (الْتِهابُ الشَّغافِ الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحادّ) all share a clinical course that is marked by sudden onset and/or short duration. This rapid and brief presentation is a common feature of the various acute rheumatic heart disease manifestations, indicating that clinicians should anticipate an abrupt, short‑lived disease course when evaluating and managing these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 1148
retrieved_docs.content: valve pathologies, broader cardiac disease categories, diagnostic modalities and therapeutic interventions is essential for accurate diagnosis, optimal treatment planning and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 1093
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic pancarditis and acute rheumatic valvulitis are both described as having a clinical course marked by sudden onset and/or short duration. This pattern of abrupt, brief presentation also characterizes the clinical course of الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحاد (acute rheumatic pericarditis) and الْتِهابُ الشَّغافِ الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحادّ (acute rheumatic myocarditis). In addition, the same sudden‑onset, short‑duration course applies to احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ الحادّ للجدار الأمامي (acute formation of the anterior wall of the heart muscle). Together, these relationships indicate that various acute rheumatic cardiac manifestations share a rapid and brief clinical presentation, which is important for prompt recognition and appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 1056
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic pancarditis and acute rheumatic valvulitis are both rheumatic heart conditions that typically present with a sudden onset and/or short duration clinical course. The descriptor “Sudden onset AND/OR short duration” is also identified as the clinical course for الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُور الرُّومَاتزميّ الحاد (acute rheumatic pericarditis), for مرض قلبي روماتزمي حاد، غير محدد (unspecified acute rheumatic heart disease), and for احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ الحادّ للجدار الأمامي (acute anterior‑wall myocardial injury). Thus, a rapid and brief presentation is a common feature linking these various acute rheumatic cardiac manifestations and certain acute myocardial conditions, underscoring the importance of recognizing this pattern for timely diagnosis and appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2832
retrieved_docs.content: The cardiac disorder (اضطراب قلبي) is clinically associated with coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي), indicating that patients with coronary atherosclerosis frequently exhibit cardiac abnormalities and that the presence of a cardiac disorder may suggest underlying coronary artery disease, a relationship that is important for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2644
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic fever (الحُمَّى الروماتيزمية) is clinically associated with the need for aortic valve replacement (استبدال الصمام الأبهري) and coronary valve replacement (استبدال الصمام التاجي). The inflammatory damage caused by rheumatic fever can affect heart valves, leading to severe dysfunction of the aortic and coronary valves that may require surgical replacement. Recognizing this relationship enables clinicians to monitor patients with rheumatic fever for progressive valvular disease and to plan timely surgical intervention, thereby improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2510
retrieved_docs.content: Acute hypernatremia, acute rheumatic carditis, acute rheumatic valvulitis, الْتِهابُ الشَّغافِ الرُّوْمَاتزميّ الحادّ (acute rheumatic myocarditis), مرض قلبي روماتزمي حاد، غير محدد (unspecified acute rheumatic heart disease), and احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ الحادّ للجدار الأمامي (acute involvement of the anterior wall of the heart) are all conditions whose clinical course is characterized by a sudden onset and/or short duration. This shared pattern of rapid and brief presentation underscores the need for prompt recognition and timely therapeutic intervention across these diverse acute disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1770
retrieved_docs.content: الْتِهابُ التَّأَمُوْر (pericarditis) is clinically associated with مخطط كهربية القلب غير طبيعي (an abnormal electrocardiogram), indicating that patients with pericardial inflammation frequently exhibit abnormal ECG findings, which are important for diagnosis and guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2914
retrieved_docs.content: Chest pain (ألم الصَدْر) is clinically associated with cardiac catheterization (قسطرة قلبية). In clinical practice, the presence of chest pain often prompts consideration of cardiac catheterization to evaluate coronary artery disease, while the procedure itself may produce chest discomfort as a post‑procedural symptom. Recognizing this association helps clinicians diagnose underlying cardiac pathology and manage patient symptoms before, during, and after the intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2049
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rheumatic carditis, acute rheumatic pancarditis, and acute rheumatic valvulitis are all described as having a clinical course that is marked by sudden onset and/or short duration. The same rapid‑onset, brief‑duration pattern is also attributed to التهاب عضل القلب الروماتزمي الحاد (acute rheumatic myocarditis) and مرض قلبي روماتزمي حاد، غير محدد (an unspecified acute rheumatic heart disease). Collectively, these forms of acute rheumatic heart disease share the characteristic of a swift and often brief clinical presentation, which is important for timely diagnosis and management.
